From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of organisation

A **social enterprise** is an organisation that applies commercial strategies to maximise improvements in financial, social and environmental well-being.[[1]](#cite_note-pmmathew2008-1) Profit made through fundraising or the sale of products is reinvested in the organisation's mission,[[2]](#cite_note-:6-2) making them more sustainable than a [non-profit organisation](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") that may solely rely on grant money, donations or [government policies](/wiki/Government_policies "Government policies").[[3]](#cite_note-3) A social enterprise's main purpose is to promote, encourage, and make [social change](/wiki/Social_change "Social change")[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) in a financially sustainable way.[[2]](#cite_note-:6-2) Unlike conventional businesses, social enterprises embed social or environmental goals into their core objectives while maintaining financial sustainability.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:4-6)[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7)[[1]](#cite_note-pmmathew2008-1) They aim to create social value while generating income, reinvesting profits into their mission rather than relying solely on philanthropy. Social enterprises can provide income-generating opportunities to address basic needs, particularly for people living in poverty. They combine financial viability with a social purpose and can expand or replicate their models to increase impact. They are often considered more sustainable than non-profit organisations, which may rely heavily on donations or government support.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

### Origins and definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins and definitions")]

One of the first examples of a social enterprise, in the form of a social cooperative, can be traced back to the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era").[[8]](#cite_note-:23-8) Like social cooperatives, social enterprises are believed[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] to have emerged as a result of state and [market failure](/wiki/Market_failure "Market failure"). However, market failure is emphasised[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] in the UK, while state failure is emphasised in the United States.[[9]](#cite_note-:154-9) Contemporary social enterprises are viewed to have been created as a result of the evolution of non-profits.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first description of a social enterprise as a democratically owned and run trading organisation that is financially independent, has social objectives and operates in an environmentally responsible way, was put forward by Freer Spreckley[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) in the UK in 1978 and later written as a publication in 1981.[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7) It was developed as an alternative commercial organisational model to private businesses, co-operatives, and public enterprises.[[6]](#cite_note-:4-6) The concept, at that time, had five main principles[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7) divided into three values and two paradigm shifts. The three values, now referred to as the [triple bottom line](/wiki/Triple_bottom_line "Triple bottom line") were:[[12]](#cite_note-spreckleytoolkit1981-12)

* Trading and financially viable independence
* Creating social wealth
* Operating in environmentally responsible ways

The two paradigm shifts were:[[12]](#cite_note-spreckleytoolkit1981-12)

* A [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership") legal structure where members/owners have one voting share and different forms of investment
* Democratic governance, where each worker/community resident is a member with one vote

It was intended as part of the original concept that social enterprises should plan, measure and report on financial performance, social-wealth creation, and environmental responsibility by the use of a [social accounting and audit](/wiki/Social_accounting_and_audit "Social accounting and audit") system.[[7]](#cite_note-auto-7)

Social enterprise has [philanthropic](/wiki/Philanthropy "Philanthropy") roots in the United States and [cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative") roots in Europe and Asia. In the US, the term is associated with 'doing charity by doing trade', rather than 'doing charity *while* doing trade'. In other countries, there is a much stronger emphasis on [community organising](/wiki/Community_organizing "Community organizing"), democratic control of capital, and mutual principles than on philanthropy.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Types and differences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Types and differences")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Social_enterprise "Special:EditPage/Social enterprise") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Social enterprise"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Social+enterprise%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Social+enterprise%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Social+enterprise%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Social+enterprise%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Social+enterprise%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Social+enterprise%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Social enterprises can be structured as a business, a partnership for profit, or a [non-profit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization"). Depending on in which country the entity exists and the legal forms available, they may take the form of a [co-operative](/wiki/Co-operative "Co-operative"), [mutual organisation](/wiki/Mutual_organization "Mutual organization"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] a [disregarded entity](/wiki/Entity_classification_election "Entity classification election"),[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) a [social business](/wiki/Social_business "Social business"), a [benefit corporation](/wiki/Benefit_corporation "Benefit corporation"), a [community interest company](/wiki/Community_interest_company "Community interest company"), a [company limited by guarantee](/wiki/Company_limited_by_guarantee "Company limited by guarantee"), or a [charity organisation](/wiki/Charity_organisation "Charity organisation"). They can also take more conventional structures.[*[example needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:AUDIENCE "Wikipedia:AUDIENCE")*] Social enterprises are dynamic, requiring adaptation to ensure they meet the needs of communities and individuals in an ever-changing world. Their shared common thread is that they all operate to achieve a balanced financial, social and environmental set of objectives. Types of social enterprises includes trading enterprises, financial institutions, community organisations, and NGOs and charities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Trading enterprises are worker- and employee-owned trading enterprises, co-operatives, and collectives. These vary from very large enterprises—such as [John Lewis Partnership](/wiki/John_Lewis_Partnership "John Lewis Partnership") in the UK and the [Mondragon Corporation](/wiki/Mondragon_Corporation "Mondragon Corporation") in Spain—to medium-sized enterprises owned by their staff with traditional management hierarchies and pay differentials, to small worker cooperatives with only a few directors and employees who work in less hierarchical ways and practice wage parity. Within the trading enterprises, there are employee-owned enterprises and membership-owned enterprises.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Examples of membership organisations registered as social enterprises are community enterprises, [housing co-operatives](/wiki/Housing_co-operative "Housing co-operative"), community interest companies with [asset locks](/wiki/Asset_lock "Asset lock"), community centres, pubs and shops, associations, housing associations, and football clubs. These usually exist for a specific purpose and trade commercially. All operate to reinvest profits in the community. They have large memberships that are customers or supporters of the organisation's key purpose. There are village cooperatives in India and Pakistan that were established as far back as 1904.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Organisations that do not take the distinct form of either a private, public, or non-profit organisation are classified as hybrid organisations.[[14]](#cite_note-:82-14) For legal and tax purposes, hybrid forms are classified as for-profit entities. The most common types of hybrid organisations are the [low-profit limited liability company](/wiki/Low-profit_limited_liability_company "Low-profit limited liability company") (L3C) and the [benefit corporation](/wiki/Benefit_corporation "Benefit corporation") (B-Corp). L3C's main objective is to achieve socially beneficial goals and do so by employing the financial and flexible advantages of a limited liability company. States in the United States that have authorised the use of the L3C model have established three requirements: to operate for charitable or educational purposes, not the production of income, and not the fulfilment of a political or legislative agenda. A benefit corporation, or B-Corp, is a corporation that operates to achieve or create a "general public benefit".[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

Many non-governmental organisations (NGOs) and charities operate commercial consulting and training enterprises or subsidiary trading enterprises, such as [Oxfam International](/wiki/Oxfam_International "Oxfam International"). The profits are used to provide salaries for people who provide free services to specific groups of people or to further the social or environmental aims of the organisation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] While not all social enterprises are non-profits, and vice versa, some social enterprises operate in a non-profit form. They earn income for their goods or services and are typically regarded as non-profits that use business strategies to generate revenue to support their charitable missions.[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15)

The organisational and legal principles embedded in social enterprises have, in some instances, come from non-profit organisations. Non-profit organisations rely on governmental and public support, but can struggle to obtain funding and, as result, have started to rely on profits from their own social change operations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The methods by which social enterprises create sustainable revenue streams differ between social businesses, but all share the goal of abandoning the need for government or donor support. [Gregory Dees](/wiki/Gregory_Dees "Gregory Dees") and Beth Anderson discuss this difference in funding strategies as the innovation that differentiates the social enterprise from the traditional non-profit actor.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Social Enterprise Alliance (SEA) identified four factors that they believe caused this transition: increase in non-profit operating costs; decline in government and public philanthropic support; increased competition due to growth in the charitable sector; and expansion in the demand for non-profit provided services.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)[[17]](#cite_note-:142-17) In 2012, survey data from non-profits in 12 countries found that donations accounted for 12% of income, with the largest source of income (43%) coming from revenue-generating activities. Some non-profits have evolved their business model to model that of a social enterprise.[[18]](#cite_note-18) This change has resulted in a type of hybrid organisation that does not have concrete organisational boundaries. Various scholars[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) have argued that this may have come about due to the marketisation of the non-profit sector, which resulted in many non-profit firms placing more focus on generating income.[[17]](#cite_note-:142-17)[*[need quotation to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[14]](#cite_note-:82-14) Other scholars have used [institutional theory](/wiki/Institutional_theory "Institutional theory") to conclude that non-profits have adopted social enterprise models, because such models have become legitimised[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] and widely accepted.[[9]](#cite_note-:154-9) Some organisations have evolved into social enterprises.[[14]](#cite_note-:82-14)

[Social impact](/wiki/Social_impact_assessment "Social impact assessment") may refer to the overall effects of a business, but a business that has social impact may or may not be a social enterprise.[[22]](#cite_note-22) [Corporate social responsibility](/wiki/Corporate_social_responsibility "Corporate social responsibility") (CSR) is a practise that businesses can use to be conscious of the social and environmental impacts of their activities. There are a variety of CSR markers, such as accountability and internal and external elements. Social enterprises place a lot of emphasis on external social responsibility as a result of their social objectives, so social impact is built into the organisation. However, there has been debate on whether or not social enterprises place enough emphasis on internal CSR, such as human resources and capital management, health and safety standards, adaptation to innovation and change, and the quality of management within the organisation.[[8]](#cite_note-:23-8) Since a large majority of social enterprises do not have sufficient funding, they are unable to pay competitive wages to their employees, and, as a result, have to resort to other, non-financial techniques to recruit employees. Many managers utilise the social component of the social enterprise's dual mission and purpose for this.[[14]](#cite_note-:82-14) Financial institutions are savings and loan organisations such as [credit unions](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union"), [microcredit](/wiki/Microcredit "Microcredit") organisations, [cooperative banks](/wiki/Cooperative_banking "Cooperative banking"), and [revolving loan funds](/wiki/Revolving_loan_fund "Revolving loan fund"), and are membership-owned social enterprises. Credit unions were first established in the 1850s in Germany and spread internationally. Cooperative banks have likewise been around since the 1870s, owned as a subsidiary of a membership co-operative. In recent times, microcredit organisations have sprung up in many developing countries to great effect. Local currency exchanges and social value exchanges are also being established.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Socially responsible investing](/wiki/Socially_responsible_investing "Socially responsible investing") (SRI) seeks to maximise both financial gain and social impact.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Many commercial enterprises would consider themselves to have social objectives, but commitment to these objectives is motivated by the perception that such commitment will ultimately make the enterprise more financially valuable. These are organisations that might be more properly said to be operating corporate responsibility policies. Social enterprises differ in that their commitment to impact is central to the mission of the business.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Like social enterprise, [social entrepreneurship](/wiki/Social_entrepreneurship "Social entrepreneurship") has a variety of existing definitions. Currently,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] there is not a widely accepted standard definition for the term, and descriptions vary in level of detail. There is an emphasis on change agents for social entrepreneurship, in contrast to the organisational focus of social enterprises. Social entrepreneurship usually takes place in the non-profit sector, with a focus on creating and implementing new solutions.[[15]](#cite_note-:3-15)

Tensions between social missions and business ventures within a social enterprise are separated into four distinct categories: performing, organising, belonging, and learning.[[23]](#cite_note-:13-23) Performance tensions arise as organisations seek to fulfil various conflicting goals, such as varying stakeholder demands, social mission goals, and performance metrics. A major challenge is figuring out how to gauge success with conflicting goals. Organising tensions are caused by inconsistencies in organisational structure, culture, and human resource practices. Many social organisations grapple with whom to hire, as many want to help disadvantaged people but also need workers with business skills to ensure the success of the enterprise. Organisations face the challenge of deciding which organisational structure and legal form (e.g., non-Profit, for-profit) to operate under. Belonging tensions arise from identification or a sense of belonging to contrasting goals and values, which creates internal organisational conflict. These tensions are amplified by the maintenance of relationships with stakeholders who may have conflicting identities within the organisation.[[23]](#cite_note-:13-23) Learning tensions are a result of conflicting time horizons (i.e., short-term vs. long-run). In the short term, organisations aim for stability, which can be evaluated based on metrics such as costs, profits, and revenues, but in the long run, they want growth, flexibility, and progress in achieving their social mission.[[23]](#cite_note-:13-23)

### Academia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Academia")]

When social enterprise first emerged, much of the scholarly literature focused on defining the key characteristics and definitions of social enterprise. Since then, there is more of a focus on the emergence of the social enterprise sector, as well as the internal management of social enterprise organisations. Due to the dual-purpose missions of social enterprises, organisations cannot directly employ the typical management strategies of established business models. Recent[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] academic literature has argued against prior positively held views of social enterprises success in striking a balance between the two tensions and instead argued that the social mission is being compromised in favour of financial stability. Prioritising social good over financial stability contradicts rational firm management, which typically prioritises financial and profit-seeking goals. As a result, different management issues arise that range from stakeholders (and management) agreeing on the firm's goals but disagreeing on an action plan to management and stakeholders disagreeing on the firm's goals. Some social enterprises have taken on same-sector and cross-sector partnerships, while others continue to operate independently.[[14]](#cite_note-:82-14)

The first academic paper to propose worker co-operatives involved in health and rehabilitation work as a form social enterprise was published in 1993.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The scale and integration of co-operative development in the 'red belt' of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") (some 7,000 worker, and 8,000 social co-operatives) inspired the formation of the EMES network of social economy researchers who subsequently spread the language to the UK and the rest of Europe through influential English language publications.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The first international social enterprise journal was established in 2005 by Social Enterprise London (with support from the London Development Association). The [*Social Enterprise Journal*](/wiki/Social_Enterprise_London#Activities "Social Enterprise London") has been followed by the *Journal of Social Entrepreneurship*, and coverage of issues pertaining to the [social economy](/wiki/Social_economy "Social economy") and social enterprise is also covered by the *[Journal of Co-operative Studies](/wiki/Journal_of_Co-operative_Studies "Journal of Co-operative Studies")* and the *Annals of Co-operative and Public Economics*. The European Social Enterprise Research Network (EMES) and the Co-operative Research Unit (CRU) at the [Open University](/wiki/Open_University "Open University") have also published research into social enterprise. The Skoll World Forum, organised jointly by [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford_University "Oxford University") and [Duke](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University") universities, brings together researchers and practitioners from across the globe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the United States, [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") and [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") universities built on the work of [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka_(non-profit_organization) "Ashoka (non-profit organization)") and contributed to the development of the social entrepreneurship field through project initiatives and publications.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit), the field of social enterprise studies had not yet developed firm philosophical foundations, but its advocates and academic community were becoming more engaged with critical pedagogies, such as that of [Paulo Freire](/wiki/Paulo_Freire "Paulo Freire"), and critical traditions in research, like [critical theory](/wiki/Critical_theory "Critical theory"), [institutional theory](/wiki/Institutional_theory "Institutional theory") and [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"), in comparison to private-sector business education.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Teaching related to the [social economy](/wiki/Social_economy "Social economy") draws explicitly from the works of [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen"), [Proudhon](/wiki/Pierre-Joseph_Proudhon "Pierre-Joseph Proudhon"), and [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), with works by [Bourdieu](/wiki/Pierre_Bourdieu "Pierre Bourdieu") and [Putnam](/wiki/Robert_D._Putnam "Robert D. Putnam") informing the debate over [social capital](/wiki/Social_capital "Social capital") and its relationship to the competitive advantage of [mutuals](/wiki/Mutual_organization "Mutual organization"). This intellectual foundation, however, does not extend as strongly into the field of social entrepreneurship, where there is more influence from writings on [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") and entrepreneurship by [Joseph Schumpeter](/wiki/Joseph_Schumpeter "Joseph Schumpeter") in conjunction with the emerging fields of [social innovation](/wiki/Social_innovation "Social innovation"), [actor-network theory](/wiki/Actor%E2%80%93network_theory "Actor–network theory"), and [complexity theory](/wiki/Complex_systems "Complex systems") to explain its processes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Social enterprise, unlike private enterprise, is not taught exclusively in a business school context, as it is increasingly connected to the health sector and to public service delivery. However, Oxford University's [Said Business School](/wiki/Said_Business_School "Said Business School") does host the [Skoll World Forum](/wiki/Skoll_World_Forum "Skoll World Forum"), a global event focused on [social entrepreneurs](/wiki/Social_entrepreneurs "Social entrepreneurs").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Africa")]

### Kenya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kenya")]

In Kenya, many NGOs use business models to improve the lives of people, mainly in rural Kenya. [KOMAZA](/wiki/KOMAZA "KOMAZA") is a social enterprise that plants trees with [smallholder](/wiki/Smallholding "Smallholding") farmers and uses [economies of scale](/wiki/Economies_of_scale "Economies of scale") to enable them to access high value markets for processed trees. Another organisation, RISE Kenya, runs projects to [mitigate climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") in the semiarid [Eastern Province](/wiki/Eastern_Province_(Kenya) "Eastern Province (Kenya)") of Kenya. They also run [weaving](/wiki/Weaving "Weaving") projects whereby women who would traditionally engage in weaving make products that are marketed in [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi") and in European and American markets.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other social enterprises in Kenya include the One Acre Fund, Nuru International and [M-Pesa](/wiki/M-Pesa "M-Pesa"), the latter of which facilitates economic transactions via mobile phone.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The organisation [Alive & Kicking](/wiki/Alive_%26_Kicking_(social_enterprise) "Alive & Kicking (social enterprise)") has produced over 200,000 sports balls from its stitching centre in Nairobi.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Social enterprises iHub and NaiLab are centres for technological enterprise, with ventures such as Tandaa in cooperation with the [ICT Board of Kenya](/wiki/Information_and_Communication_Technology_Authority "Information and Communication Technology Authority") and Akirachix.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Zambia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Zambia")]

Social enterprises in Zambia are often focused on the creation of sustainable employment.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Alive & Kicking](/wiki/Alive_%26_Kicking_(social_enterprise) "Alive & Kicking (social enterprise)") established a stitching centre in [Lusaka](/wiki/Lusaka "Lusaka") in 2007, which employs 50 stitchers and produces 2,000 sports balls a month.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Zambikes produces a range of bicycles from their Lusaka factory, including 'Zambulances' and ones made from [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo"), and provide three levels of mechanic training.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Asia")]

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: China")]

Researcher Meng Zhao states that social enterprise as a concept emerged in China around 2012, although it was not yet a well-known idea among the general public, and the Chinese government was still "trying to understand the new phenomenon".[[34]](#cite_note-zhao-34) He identified three forms in China: the social enterprise; the social startup; and the startup for social good. The terms "startup" is used because it carries some of the spirit associated with "enterprise" in English, such as innovation, risk taking or "venture".[[34]](#cite_note-zhao-34)

There is no separate legal entity for social enterprises in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"). They are normally registered as companies or non-profit organisations. The Hong Kong Government defines social enterprises as businesses that achieve specific social objectives, and its profits will be principally reinvested in the business for the social objectives that it pursues, rather than distribution to its shareholders.[[35]](#cite_note-35) In recent years, [venture philanthropy](/wiki/Venture_philanthropy "Venture philanthropy") organisations, such as Social Ventures Hong Kong and Social Enterprise Business Centre of the [Hong Kong Council of Social Service](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Council_of_Social_Service "Hong Kong Council of Social Service"), have been set up to invest in viable social enterprises with a significant social impact.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: India")]

In India, a social enterprise may be a [non-profit NGO](/wiki/Non-governmental_organisations_in_India "Non-governmental organisations in India"), often registered as a Society under the [Societies Registration Act, 1860](/wiki/Societies_Registration_Act,_1860 "Societies Registration Act, 1860"); a Trust registered under various Indian State [Trust Acts](/wiki/Indian_Trusts_Act,_1882 "Indian Trusts Act, 1882"); or a Section 25 Company registered under [Companies Act 1956](/wiki/Companies_Act_1956 "Companies Act 1956"). India has around three million[[36]](#cite_note-36) NGOs, including a number of religious organisations and trusts, like temples, mosques and [gurudwara](/wiki/Gurudwara "Gurudwara") associations, who are not considered social enterprises.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the agricultural sector, [International Development Enterprises](/wiki/International_Development_Enterprises "International Development Enterprises") has helped pull millions of small farmers out of poverty in India.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Another area of social enterprise in India and the [developing world](/wiki/Developing_world "Developing world") are [bottom of the pyramid](/wiki/Bottom_of_the_pyramid "Bottom of the pyramid") (BOP) businesses, which were identified and analysed by [C. K. Prahalad](/wiki/C._K._Prahalad "C. K. Prahalad") in *[Fortune at the Base of the Pyramid](/wiki/The_Fortune_at_the_Bottom_of_the_Pyramid "The Fortune at the Bottom of the Pyramid")*.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The term "social enterprise" is not widely used in India. Instead, terms like NGOs and NPOs (non-profit organisations) are used, where these kind of organisations are legally allowed to raise fund for non-business activities. NGOs in India raise funds through some services, often fund raising events and community activities, and occasionally products. [Child Rights and You](/wiki/Child_Rights_and_You "Child Rights and You") and [Youth United](/wiki/Youth_United "Youth United") are examples of social enterprises that raise funds through their services, fund-raising activities (organising events, donations, and grants) or products to further their social and environmental goals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] According to SOPAR-Canada's sister organisation Bala Vikasa Social Service Society, "Social Enterprise is a hybrid business with a goal of solving social problems, while also generating revenues and profits like any other enterprise. However, when it comes to choosing between profits or social cause, social cause is paramount for social enterprises, while profits are considered only for sustainability."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

Social Enterprise Alliance Malaysia defines social enterprises as "organizations created to address social problems that use business models to sustain themselves financially...[they] seek to create not only financial returns but also social returns to their beneficiaries."[[38]](#cite_note-38) In Malaysia, government bodies, like [MaGIC](/wiki/Malaysian_Global_Innovation_and_Creativity_Centre "Malaysian Global Innovation and Creativity Centre"), seek to strengthen Malaysia's position as an emerging innovation nation.[[39]](#cite_note-39) One of MaGIC's key missions is "nurturing and navigating local startup and social enterprise into successful and sustainable businesses". One initiative by MaGIC in 2017 was the Impact Driven Enterprise Accreditation (IDEA). MaGIC also launched Buy For Impact, which encourages companies to purchase products or services from social enterprises. This initiative promotes conscious buying behaviours among the general public and the private sector, stressing the importance and impact of purchasing power.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On April 23, 2022, Prime Minister [Ismail Sabri Yaakob](/wiki/Ismail_Sabri_Yaakob "Ismail Sabri Yaakob") outlined a new direction for the country on social entrepreneurship development in his report, *Social Entrepreneurship Action Framework 2030 (SEMy2030)*.[[40]](#cite_note-40) SEMy2030 will provide more structured training on the adaptation of technology and digitalisation, widen access to financing and financial support, and provide access to the domestic and international markets.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Philippines")]

In December 1999, a group of entrepreneurs, executives, and academics in [Metro Manila](/wiki/Metro_Manila "Metro Manila") started the Social Enterprise Network (SEN), which serves as a networking opportunity for like-minded individuals to share their interests and experience. One of its projects, the Cooperative Marketing Enterprise (CME), was eventually adopted by the Foundations for People Development. CME is devoted solely to providing the need for cooperatives, micro, small, and medium enterprises for the marketing of their products.

The course Social Entrepreneurship and Management was first offered at the [University of Asia and the Pacific](/wiki/University_of_Asia_and_the_Pacific "University of Asia and the Pacific") School of Management in 2000. This course was developed and taught by Dr. Jose Rene C. Gayo, then the Dean of the School of Management. It was offered as an elective for the senior students of the Bachelor of Science in Entrepreneurial Management. In March 2001, the university held a seminar called Social Enterprises: Creating Wealth for the Poor.

One social enterprise in the Philippines is GKonomics International, Inc., a non-stock, non-profit organisation incorporated in 2009. They are a [Gawad Kalinga](/wiki/Gawad_Kalinga "Gawad Kalinga") partner in social enterprise development aiming to build a new generation of producers.

### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: South Korea")]

The majority of Korean social enterprises are primarily concerned with job creation.[[42]](#cite_note-parkwilding-42) The Social Enterprise Promotion Act was approved in December 2006 and put into effect in July 2007. It was amended in 2010.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Article 2 defines a social enterprise as "an organization which is engaged in business activities of producing and selling goods and services while pursuing a social purpose of enhancing the quality of local residents' life" by providing social services and battling job scarcity. These social services include "education, health, social welfare, environment and culture and other service proportionate to this."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Ministry of Labor](/wiki/Ministry_of_Labor_(South_Korea) "Ministry of Labor (South Korea)") establishes a Basic Plan for Social Enterprises Support every five years, as decreed in Article 5. Enterprises, cooperatives, and non-profits can all be recognised as social enterprises, which are eligible for tax reduction and/or financial supports from the national, provincial, or local governments. 680 entities were recognised as social enterprises as of October 2012.[[42]](#cite_note-parkwilding-42) The Korea Social Enterprise Promotion Agency was established to promote social enterprises, which include Happynarae and [Beautiful Store](/wiki/Beautiful_Store "Beautiful Store").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Thailand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Thailand")]

In Thailand, social entrepreneurship is small but growing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Thammasat University](/wiki/Thammasat_University "Thammasat University") in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") is the Southeast Asia partner of the Global Social Venture Competition (GSVC-SEA).[[44]](#cite_note-44) Every year, emerging social enterprises present their business models, which range from agriculture to technology, tourism and education. In 2013, the winners of GSVC-SEA were [Wedu Global](/wiki/Wedu_Global "Wedu Global") and the agricultural organisation CSA Munching box.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The government of Thailand supports the creation of new social enterprises via the Thai Social Entrepreneurship office (TSEO).[[45]](#cite_note-45) The [Royal Family](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Thailand "Monarchy of Thailand") became involved in social entrepreneurship through brand creations such as the [Mae Fah Luang Foundation](/wiki/Mae_Fah_Luang_Foundation "Mae Fah Luang Foundation")'s Doi Tung.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Major social enterprises in Thailand include ChangeFusion, led by the [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka:_Innovators_for_the_Public "Ashoka: Innovators for the Public") Fellow Sunit Shrestha;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Singha Park Chiangrai,[[46]](#cite_note-46) which hired 1200 local unemployed people;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and [Mechai Viravaidya](/wiki/Mechai_Viravaidya "Mechai Viravaidya")'s[[47]](#cite_note-47) [Population and Community Development Association](/wiki/Population_and_Community_Development_Association "Population and Community Development Association") (PDA).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Central/South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Central/South America")]

### Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Chile")]

Social enterprises in Chile adopt various forms, like cooperatives, associations, private limited companies or corporations. The [Ministry of Economy](/wiki/Ministry_of_Economy,_Development,_and_Tourism_(Chile) "Ministry of Economy, Development, and Tourism (Chile)") developed a legal form used to establish the rights and duties for social enterprises.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Chile's Productivity, Innovation and Growth Agenda, which has 47 measures, 10 bills and 37 administrative initiatives, invested US$1.5 million into programmes to support social enterprises between 2014 and 2018.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Chilean government's [Chilean Economic Development Agency](/wiki/CORFO "CORFO") (CORFO) initiative helped implement programmes like the Social Innovation Program and the Seed Subsidy for Flexible Asignation to Support Social Innovation Start-up Program, both of which have provided [seed capital](/wiki/Seed_capital "Seed capital") to [incubators](/wiki/Business_incubator "Business incubator") supporting social entrepreneurs. The Ministry of Social Development also promoted grant-matching funds, like Más por Chile[[49]](#cite_note-49) and Incubia Fund to support the development programmes aiming to reduce poverty and strengthen youth.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Definition")]

The European research network, EMES, uses a more [Weberian](/wiki/Max_Weber "Max Weber") '[ideal type](/wiki/Ideal_type "Ideal type")' definition for social enterprises, rather than a prescriptive definition. This relies on nine criteria in two categories:[[50]](#cite_note-50)

* Economic criteria:
  + Continuous production and/or sale of goods and services (rather than predominantly advisory or grant-giving functions)
  + High level of autonomy from public authorities or private companies, even if they may benefit from grants and donations. Their members have the right to participate ('voice') and to leave the organisation ('exit').
  + Significant economic risk dependent on the efforts of their members
  + Minimum number of paid workers
* Social criteria:
  + Explicit aim of community benefit, including the promotion of a sense of social responsibility at the local level
  + Citizen initiative
  + Decision-making not based on capital ownership
  + Participatory character, involving those affected by the activity. In many cases, one of the objectives is to strengthen democracy at local level through economic activity.
  + Limited distribution of profit to avoid profit-maximising behaviour

Meanwhile, as part of its Social Business Initiative,[[51]](#cite_note-51) which ran from 2011 until 2014, the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") developed its own definition based on three key criteria: social objective, limited profit distribution, and participatory governance. Here, social enterprises are defined as "an operator in the social economy whose main objective is to have a social impact rather than make a profit for their owners or shareholders." It uses entrepreneurship and innovation to provide and market goods and services, and uses its profits primarily to achieve social objectives. Additionally, it is "managed in an open and responsible manner and, in particular, involve[s] employees, consumers and stakeholders affected by its commercial activities."[[52]](#cite_note-52)

The Commission uses the term 'social enterprise' to cover the following types of business:[[53]](#cite_note-:5-53)

* Those for which the social or societal objective of the common good is the reason for the commercial activity, often in the form of a high level of social innovation
* Those where profits are mainly reinvested with a view to achieving this social objective
* Those where the method of organisation or ownership system reflects their mission, using democratic or participatory principles or focusing on social justice

This definition is inclusive of businesses providing social services and/or goods and services to vulnerable persons (access to housing, health care, assistance for elderly or disabled persons, inclusion of vulnerable groups, child care, access to employment and training, dependency management, etc.); and of businesses with a method of production of goods or services with a social objective (social and professional integration via access to employment for people disadvantaged in particular by insufficient qualifications or social or professional problems leading to exclusion and marginalisation) but whose activity may be outside the realm of the provision of social goods or services.[[53]](#cite_note-:5-53)

### Czech Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Czech Republic")]

In the Czech Republic, a working party stemming from the development partnerships in the [EQUAL](/wiki/EQUAL_Community_Initiative "EQUAL Community Initiative") programme agreed on specific distinctions in April 2008. They defined social entrepreneurship as an organisation that "develops independent business activities and is active on the market in order to solve issues of employment, social coherence and local development. Its activities support solidarity, social inclusion and growth of social capital mainly on local level with the maximum respect of sustainable development."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This includes complementary activities of NGOs destined to reinvest profit into the main public benefit activity of an organisation. The group stipulated that the broad definition of social entrepreneurship should not be directly bound to legal benefits and financial support because the concept of social entrepreneurship might be threatened by misuse and disintegration. Conditions of eventual legal and financial support should be discussed by experts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The legal form a social enterprise takes may not always be seen as important, however, they must be subject to private law. According to the existing legal system, they can function in a form of cooperatives, civic associations, public benefit associations, church legal entities, Ltd., stock companies, and sole traders. Budgetary organisations and municipalities are not social enterprises as they are not autonomous, but parts of public administration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

These definitions stem from the four basic principles[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] the group believes should be followed by social enterprises. These standards were settled as the minimum so that they should be observed by all legal entities and all types of social enterprises. Specific types of enterprises that are undergoing pilot verification within CIP EQUAL projects and that are already functioning in the Czech Republic are social firms employing seriously disadvantaged target groups, and municipal social cooperatives as a suitable form of entrepreneurship with the view of development of local communities and microregions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A social economy is a "complex of autonomous private activities realised by different types of organisations that have the aim to serve their members or local community first of all by doing business." They tend to focus on solving issues of unemployment, social coherence and local development, and pour any profit back into the community. Social economies are created and developed using the [triple bottom line](/wiki/Triple_bottom_line "Triple bottom line"). Citizens are encouraged to become involved and internal relations stress the inclusion of members and/or employees in decision-making and self-management.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Finland")]

In Finland, a law was passed in 2004 that defines a social enterprise (*sosiaalinen yritys*) as any sort of enterprise that is entered on the relevant register and at least 30% of whose employees are disabled or long-term unemployed. As of March 2007, 91 such enterprises had been registered, the largest with 50 employees. In the UK, the more specific term "social firm" is used to distinguish such "integration enterprises". This legal definition of a social enterprise made it difficult for actual social entrepreneurship to enter the Finnish consciousness and public debate, so a new term, *yhteiskunnallinen yrittäjyys* (societal entrepreneurship), was dubbed and promoted by the early players in the field. Nowadays, the term is recognised, accepted and even promoted by entrepreneurial NGOs, entrepreneurs themselves, co-operatives, and government organisations. The Finnish Social Enterprise Research Network (FinSERN) collects and exchanges national and international research data, maintains connections with social enterprise researchers and research networks around the world, and finds financing opportunities for research. There is also a growing interest in [impact investment](/wiki/Impact_investment "Impact investment") in Finland.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Italy")]

Italy passed a law in 2005 on *[imprese sociali](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/impresa_sociale "it:impresa sociale")*, to which the government has given form and definition by Legislative Decree no. 155, dated 24 March 2006. Under Italian law, a social enterprise is a private entity that provides social utility goods and services, acting for the common interest and not for profit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In an effort to develop social enterprises and measure social impact, the Italian governmental work placement agency, Italia Lavoro, has developed a method to calculate the social efficiency of their project, from an economic point of view. For example, they measure the economic value to the society of providing a job to a disabled person. Since 1997, Italia Lavoro has provided work placements to people with health problems or mental and physical disabilities and to those who are [socially disadvantaged](/wiki/Socially_disadvantaged "Socially disadvantaged"). To this aim, they help people who have fallen through the cracks of the general work system to reintegrate themselves into society through the creation of small and medium non-profit enterprises.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Also intended to generate more social enterprises is the non-profit cooperative Make a Change, which provides financial, operational and management support to social start-ups. In 2010, they organised the first edition of a contest to elect the Social Entrepreneur of the Year. This year's winner[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] was the social cooperative Cauto, which manages the entire trash life-cycle in the [Province of Brescia](/wiki/Province_of_Brescia "Province of Brescia"). One-third of Cauto's workers are disabled or disadvantaged. Another of Make a Change's contests is called The World's Most Beautiful Job.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2010, the Tavern of the Good and Bad project by Domus de luna from [Cagliari](/wiki/Cagliari "Cagliari") won the competition. The tavern employs mums and children recently graduated from rehabilitation programmes. The prize consisted of a grant of €30,000 and 12 months of professional consulting and support. The awards ceremony was included in the programme of the Global Entrepreneurship Week.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

[Empresas de inserción](/wiki/Empresa_de_insercion "Empresa de insercion") are a type of social enterprise in Spain.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In the UK, the accepted definition of social enterprise is a "business with primarily social objectives whose surpluses are principally reinvested for that purpose." This definition comes from the 2002 [Department of Trade and Industry](/wiki/Department_of_Trade_and_Industry_(United_Kingdom) "Department of Trade and Industry (United Kingdom)") report *Social Enterprise: a strategy for success* and is used by sector bodies such as [Social Enterprise UK](/wiki/Social_Enterprise_UK "Social Enterprise UK") and Social Enterprise Mark CIC.[[57]](#cite_note-57) In the British context, social enterprises include community enterprises, [credit unions](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union"), trading arms of [charities](/wiki/Charities "Charities"), [employee-owned businesses](/wiki/Employee-owned_business "Employee-owned business"), [co-operatives](/wiki/Co-operatives "Co-operatives"), [development trusts](/wiki/Development_trust "Development trust"), [housing associations](/wiki/Housing_association "Housing association"), [social firms](/wiki/Social_firm "Social firm"), and leisure trusts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Whereas conventional businesses distribute their profit among [shareholders](/wiki/Shareholder "Shareholder"), in social enterprises the surplus tends to go towards one or more social aims which the business has – for example, education for the poor, vocational training for disabled people, environmental issues or for animal rights, although this may not always be the case.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Social enterprises are often seen as distinct from charities[[59]](#cite_note-59) and from private sector companies with policies on [corporate social responsibility](/wiki/Corporate_social_responsibility "Corporate social responsibility"). An emerging view, however, is that social enterprise is a particular type of trading activity that sometimes gives rise to distinct organisation forms reflecting a commitment to social cause working with stakeholders from more than one sector of the economy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Following broad consultation, the UK's Social Enterprise Unit adopted a broader definition which is independent of any legal model. This latitudinarian definition could include not only [companies limited by guarantee](/wiki/Company_limited_by_guarantee "Company limited by guarantee") and [industrial and provident societies](/wiki/Industrial_and_provident_society "Industrial and provident society") but also companies limited by shares, unincorporated associations, partnerships, and sole traders.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Freer Spreckley and Cliff Southcombe established the first[[60]](#cite_note-60) specialist support organisation in the UK Social Enterprise Partnership Ltd. in March 1997.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] They identified three common characteristics of social enterprises:

* Enterprise orientation: They are directly involved in producing goods or providing services to a market. They seek to be viable trading organisations, with an operating surplus.
* Social aims: They have explicit social aims such as job creation, training or the provision of local services. They have ethical values including a commitment to local capacity building, and they are accountable to their members and the wider community for their social environmental and economic impact.
* Social ownership: They are autonomous organisations with governance and ownership structures based on participation by stakeholder groups (users or clients, local community groups, etc.) or by trustees. Profits are distributed as profit sharing to stakeholders or used for the benefit of the community.

The UK has also developed a legal form called the [community interest company](/wiki/Community_interest_company "Community interest company") (CIC). CICs are a type of limited company designed specifically for those wishing to operate for the benefit of the community rather than for the benefit of the owners of the company. This means that a CIC cannot be formed or used solely for the personal gain of a particular person, or group of people. Legislation caps the level of dividends payable at 35% of profits and returns to individuals are capped at 4% above the bank base rate. CICs can be limited by shares, or by guarantee, and have a statutory "[asset lock](/wiki/Asset_lock "Asset lock")" to prevent the assets and profits being distributed, except as permitted by legislation. This ensures the assets and profits are retained within the CIC for community purposes, or transferred to another asset-locked organisation, such as another CIC or charity. A CIC cannot be formed to support political activities and a company that is a charity cannot be a CIC, unless it gives up its charitable status. However, a charity may apply to register a CIC as a subsidiary company.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another type of social enterprise category in the UK is a [social firm](/wiki/Social_firm "Social firm"), a business set up specifically to create employment for people otherwise severely disadvantaged in the labour market.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Organisations

A survey conducted for the Social Enterprise Unit in 2004 found that there were 15,000 social enterprises in the UK (counting only those that were incorporated as companies limited by guarantee or industrial and provident societies). This is 1.2% of all enterprises in the UK. They employed 450,000 people, of whom two-thirds were full-time, plus a further 300,000 volunteers. Their combined annual turnover is £18 billion, and the median turnover is £285,000. Of this, 84% is from trading. In 2006, the government revised this estimate upwards to 55,000, based on a survey of a sample of owners of businesses with employees, which found that 5% defined themselves as social enterprises.[[62]](#cite_note-62) A 2013 estimates suggested that there were approximately 68,000 social enterprises in the UK, contributing £24 billion to the UK economy.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Using the EU definition of social economy, the annual contribution of social enterprises to the UK economy is four times larger, at £98 billion,[[64]](#cite_note-64) because it includes the contribution of all co-operatives, mutuals and associations that produce goods or services to improve human well-being.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

British social enterprises include [Belu Water](/wiki/Belu_(company) "Belu (company)"), [The Big Issue](/wiki/The_Big_Issue "The Big Issue"), [Divine Chocolate](/wiki/Divine_Chocolate "Divine Chocolate"),[[65]](#cite_note-seguide-65) [Cafédirect](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9direct "Cafédirect"), [Camara](/wiki/Camara_(social_enterprise) "Camara (social enterprise)"), [The Co-operative Group](/wiki/The_Co-operative_Group "The Co-operative Group"), [Eden Project](/wiki/Eden_Project "Eden Project"), [Fairfield Materials Management Ltd](/wiki/Fairfield_Materials_Management_Ltd "Fairfield Materials Management Ltd"), [HCT Group](/wiki/HCT_Group "HCT Group"), [John Lewis Partnership](/wiki/John_Lewis_Partnership "John Lewis Partnership"), [Launch It](/wiki/Launch_It "Launch It"), [London Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/London_Symphony_Orchestra "London Symphony Orchestra"), [Skoll Centre for Social Entrepreneurship](/wiki/Skoll_Centre_for_Social_Entrepreneurship "Skoll Centre for Social Entrepreneurship"), [Technology Trust](/wiki/Technology_Trust "Technology Trust"), [Two Fingers Brewing Co.](/wiki/Two_Fingers_Brewing_Co. "Two Fingers Brewing Co."), and [Welsh Water](/wiki/Welsh_Water "Welsh Water").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Bodies

The first agency in the UK, [Social Enterprise London](/wiki/Social_Enterprise_London "Social Enterprise London") (SEL), was established in 1998 following collaboration between bodies supporting co-operative enterprise. It worked with the [London Development Agency](/wiki/London_Development_Agency "London Development Agency") (LDA) to establish both an undergraduate degree in social enterprise at the [University of East London](/wiki/University_of_East_London "University of East London") and a *Social Enterprise Journal*, which is now managed by [Liverpool John Moores University](/wiki/Liverpool_John_Moores_University "Liverpool John Moores University"). SEL built a network of over 2,000 social enterprises and social entrepreneurs, directly brokered over 500 social enterprise jobs under the [Department for Work and Pensions](/wiki/Department_for_Work_and_Pensions "Department for Work and Pensions")'s [Future Jobs Fund](/wiki/Future_Jobs_Fund "Future Jobs Fund"), and delivers consultancy and business support across the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] SEL is just one arm of the national [Social Enterprise UK](/wiki/Social_Enterprise_UK "Social Enterprise UK") (SEUK),[[66]](#cite_note-66) which liaises with similar groups in each region of England, as well as in Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Social Enterprise Mark CIC was the accreditation and standards body[[67]](#cite_note-67) responsible for what was previously the only internationally available social enterprise accreditation, the Social Enterprise Mark and Social Enterprise Gold Mark. It existed to recognise and promote the capabilities of social enterprises as competitive, sustainable businesses, dedicated to maximising social impact above shareholder profit. It ensured the social enterprise business model remained ethical, credible, and commercial through accreditation. More than 200 organisations held the Social Enterprise Mark/Gold Mark accreditation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2002, the [National Council for Voluntary Organisations](/wiki/National_Council_for_Voluntary_Organisations "National Council for Voluntary Organisations") (NCVO) established the Sustainable Funding Project. Using funds from [Futurebuilders England](/wiki/Futurebuilders_England "Futurebuilders England"), [Centrica](/wiki/Centrica "Centrica"), and Charity Bank, this project promoted the concept of sustainability through trading to voluntary groups and charities.[[68]](#cite_note-68) From 2005 onward, NCVO began using the term social enterprise to refer to voluntary sector trading activities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2002, the British government launched a unified Social Enterprise Strategy[[69]](#cite_note-69) and established a Social Enterprise Unit (SEnU) to co-ordinate its implementation in England and Wales, primarily to consult on a new type of company to support social enterprise development.[[70]](#cite_note-dti2003-70) The SEnU was initially established within the [Department of Trade and Industry](/wiki/Department_of_Trade_and_Industry_(United_Kingdom) "Department of Trade and Industry (United Kingdom)") (DTI), and in 2006 became part of the newly created [Office of the Third Sector](/wiki/Office_of_the_Third_Sector "Office of the Third Sector"), under the wing of the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After a consultation, the SEnU found that policy development was increasingly influenced by organisations in the conventional "non-profit" sector rather than those with their origins in employee-ownership and co-operative sectors. The 2003 DTI report on the consultation shows the disproportionate influence of charitable trusts and umbrella organisations in the voluntary sector, and evidence now exists that the voices of progressive employee-owned organisations were marginalised in the course of producing the report.[[70]](#cite_note-dti2003-70) In April 2012, Prime Minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") launched [Big Society Capital](/wiki/Big_Society_Capital "Big Society Capital"), the world's first social investment wholesaler. Capitalised with a total of £600 million, it was built to distribute funds to intermediaries that will lend money to social enterprises, charities, and community groups.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2012, [Social Enterprise UK](/wiki/Social_Enterprise_UK "Social Enterprise UK") ran the 'Not In Our Name' campaign against [Salesforce.com](/wiki/Salesforce.com "Salesforce.com"), a global software and CRM company that had begun using the term 'social enterprise' to describe its products and had applied for 'social enterprise' trademarks in the EU, US, Australia, and Jamaica. The campaign was supported by similar organisations in the US (the Social Enterprise Alliance), Canada, South Africa, and Australia. An open letter was sent to the CEO and Chairman of Salesforce.com asking Salesforce.com to stop using the term 'social enterprise'. It was signed by people and organisations around the world, including [Muhammad Yunus](/wiki/Muhammad_Yunus "Muhammad Yunus"), [Richard G. Wilkinson](/wiki/Richard_G._Wilkinson "Richard G. Wilkinson"), and [Kate Pickett](/wiki/Kate_Pickett "Kate Pickett"). Salesforce said it would withdraw applications to trademark the term 'social enterprise', and remove any references to 'social enterprise' in its marketing materials in the future.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

#### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Scotland")]

In [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), social enterprise is a devolved function and is part of the remit of the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government").[[72]](#cite_note-72) Activities are coordinated by the Scottish Social Enterprise Coalition, and intellectual leadership is provided by the Social Enterprise Institute at [Heriot-Watt University](/wiki/Heriot-Watt_University "Heriot-Watt University") in Edinburgh, established under the directorship of Declan Jones. SENScot, based in Edinburgh, supports social entrepreneurs through a variety of activities, including a weekly email bulletin by co-founder Lawrence Demarco.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The Social Enterprise Academy "deliver[s] leadership, enterprise, and social impact programmes" throughout Scotland,[[74]](#cite_note-74) and further support is provided by [Development Trusts Association Scotland](/wiki/Development_trust "Development trust") and Co-operative Development Scotland.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Middle East")]

There is no separate legal entity for, or proper definition of, social enterprises in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"). Most social enterprises register as companies or non-profit organisations.[[77]](#cite_note-77) However, social enterprises in the Middle East are active and innovating in a variety of sectors and industries. A majority of the existing social enterprises are engaged in human capital development and are training leaders with the experiences and skills needed to enhance the region's global competitiveness and social goals. These include the growing interest among youth in achieving social impact and growth in volunteerism among youth.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Among the social entrepreneurs listed on the [Schwab Foundation](/wiki/Schwab_Foundation "Schwab Foundation")'s website whose work impacted the Middle Eastern in 2013 were [Mohammad Abbad Andaloussi](/wiki/Mohammad_Abbad_Andaloussi "Mohammad Abbad Andaloussi") (Morocco), [Ibrahim Abouleish](/wiki/Ibrahim_Abouleish "Ibrahim Abouleish") (Egypt), [Eli Beer](/wiki/Eli_Beer "Eli Beer") (Israel), [Ron Bruder](/wiki/Ron_Bruder "Ron Bruder") (United States), [Jim Fruchterman](/wiki/Jim_Fruchterman "Jim Fruchterman") (United States), [Essma Ben Hamida](/wiki/Essma_Ben_Hamida "Essma Ben Hamida") (Tunisia), [Laila Iskander](/wiki/Laila_Iskander "Laila Iskander") (Egypt), [Wendy Kopp](/wiki/Wendy_Kopp "Wendy Kopp") (United States), [Roy Prosterman](/wiki/Roy_Prosterman "Roy Prosterman") (United States), [Linda Rottenberg](/wiki/Linda_Rottenberg "Linda Rottenberg") (United States), [Soraya Salti](/wiki/Soraya_Salti "Soraya Salti") (Jordan), [Tom Szaky](/wiki/Tom_Szaky "Tom Szaky") (Canada), and [Yuval Wagner](/wiki/Yuval_Wagner "Yuval Wagner") (Israel).[[79]](#cite_note-79)

## North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: North America")]

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Canada")]

The Social Enterprise Council of Canada (SECC) defines social enterprises as "businesses owned by nonprofit organizations, that is directly involved in the production and/or selling of goods and services for the blended purpose of generating income and achieving social, cultural, and/or environmental aims. Social enterprises are one more tool for non-profits to use to meet their mission to contribute to healthy communities."[[80]](#cite_note-80) Canadian social enterprise characteristics vary by region and province in the ways they differentiate social enterprises from other types of businesses, not-for-profits, co-operatives, and government agencies. Significant regional differences in legislation, financing, support agencies and corporate structures can be seen across Canada as a result of different historical development paths in the social economy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Social enterprises may directly address social needs through their products and services, the number of people they employ, or the use of their financial surplus. This can distinguish them from "socially responsible for-profit businesses", which create positive [social change](/wiki/Social_change "Social change") indirectly through the practice of [corporate social responsibility](/wiki/Corporate_social_responsibility "Corporate social responsibility") (e.g., creating and implementing a charitable foundation; paying fair wages to their employees; using environmentally friendly [raw materials](/wiki/Raw_material "Raw material"); providing volunteers to help with community projects). Social enterprises may use earned revenue strategies to pursue a [double](/wiki/Double_bottom_line "Double bottom line") or [triple bottom line](/wiki/Triple_bottom_line "Triple bottom line"), either alone (as a social economy business, in either the [private](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") or the not-for-profit sector) or as a significant part of a not-for-profit corporation's mixed income stream that may include charitable contributions and [public sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector") assistance. This distinguishes them from some traditional not-for-profit corporations, which may rely in whole or part on charitable and government support.[[8]](#cite_note-:23-8)

[Habitat for Humanity ReStore](/wiki/Habitat_for_Humanity#ReStore "Habitat for Humanity"), Eva's Print Shop, and [ME to WE](/wiki/ME_to_WE "ME to WE") are some well-known social enterprises operating in Canada.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: United States")]

The Social Enterprise Alliance defines social enterprises as "organizations that address a basic unmet need or solve a social or environmental problem through a market-driven approach."[[82]](#cite_note-82) In the U.S., two distinct characteristics differentiate social enterprises from other types of businesses, non-profits, and government agencies:

* Social enterprises directly address social needs through their products and services or through the numbers of disadvantaged people they employ[[8]](#cite_note-:23-8)
* Social enterprises use earned revenue strategies to pursue a double or triple bottom line, either alone or as part of a non-profit's mixed revenue stream. The double bottom line consists of social goals and [profit maximisation](/wiki/Profit_maximisation "Profit maximisation"). Here, the two are not contradictory; however, proper financial management to achieve positive profits is necessary in order to undertake the organisations social goals. The triple bottom line is essentially the double bottom line, with the addition of environmental sustainability.[[8]](#cite_note-:23-8) It focuses on economic vitality, environmental sustainability, and social responsibility.[[83]](#cite_note-:1-83)

In the U.S., a social enterprise is distinct from social entrepreneurship, which broadly encompasses such diverse players as [B-Corp](/wiki/B_Corporation_(certification) "B Corporation (certification)") companies, socially responsible investors, "for-benefit" ventures, [Fourth Sector organisations](/wiki/Quaternary_sector_of_the_economy "Quaternary sector of the economy"), CSR efforts by major corporations, "[social innovators](/wiki/Social_innovation "Social innovation")," and others. All these types of entities grapple with social needs in a variety of ways, but, unless they directly address social needs through their products or services or the numbers of disadvantaged people they employ, they do not qualify as social enterprises.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] According to a paper published by [De Gruyter](/wiki/De_Gruyter "De Gruyter") in 2019, some common challenges facing social enterprises in the U.S. include legal form, governance challenges, difficulties in measuring impact, lack of clear identity, problems in accessing capital, and management tensions.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Society Profits offers third-party accreditation to social enterprise businesses.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

## Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Oceania")]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Social_enterprise&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Australia")]

In Australia, a social enterprise is a business that exists specifically to "make the world a better place."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Social Enterprise National Strategy (SENS) project was started in 2020 with the aim of developing the country's "first social enterprise strategy and [to] secure Federal Government support for its implementation."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The group's advisory committee included the Australian Centre for Rural Entrepreneurship (ACRE), Social Enterprise Network Victoria (SENVIC), Social Traders, the South Australian Social Enterprise Council, YLab, Good Cycles and the English Family Foundation (EFF). Their work resulted in the foundation of Social Enterprise Australia in February 2022. This organisation was intended to "facilitate and lead the development of a national strategy" for social enterprise.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Its mission was framed around "environmental care, people-centred services, access to decent work, and community-led innovation."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A 2022 report by SENS showed that there were more than 12,000 social enterprises operating in Australia, employing more than 206,000 people and making a sum contribution of $21.3 billion to the economy.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In 2017, the [Centre for Social Impact](/wiki/Centre_for_Social_Impact "Centre for Social Impact") at [Swinburne University](/wiki/Swinburne_University "Swinburne University") undertook a comprehensive mapping project of social enterprise in Victoria.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The report identified 3,500 social enterprises in Victoria alone, employing over 60,000 people, or 1.8% of the state's workforce.[[88]](#cite_note-88) [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia")'s social enterprises contributed over $5.2 billion in gross output to the state's economy.[[89]](#cite_note-mapforimpact.com.au-89) 20% of Victoria's social enterprise workforce in 2017 was people with disability (i.e., 12,000 jobs) and 7% of jobs were held by people previously experiencing long-term unemployment.[[89]](#cite_note-mapforimpact.com.au-89)

Social Enterprise Australia's People and Planet First initiative offers "global verification," which identifies social enterprises as affordable and sustainable.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
